content: Must the burnt offering use a male or female animal?
explanation: Leviticus 1:3 — 'If his offering is a burnt offering from the herd, he shall offer a male without blemish.'

content: If a burnt offering of birds is offered, what kind of bird must be used?
explanation: Leviticus 1:14 — 'If his offering to the LORD is a burnt offering of birds, then he shall bring his offering of turtledoves or pigeons.'

content: What was the main ingredient of the grain offering?
explanation: Leviticus 2:1 — 'When anyone brings a grain offering as an offering to the LORD, his offering shall be of fine flour. He shall pour oil on it and put frankincense on it.'

content: Could a grain offering presented to the LORD contain leaven?
explanation: Leviticus 2:11 — 'No grain offering that you bring to the LORD shall be made with leaven, for you shall burn no leaven nor any honey as a food offering to the LORD.'
options: Never — no leaven or honey allowed

content: Every grain offering must be seasoned with ___.
explanation: Leviticus 2:13 — 'You shall season all your grain offerings with salt. You shall not let the salt of the covenant with your God be missing from your grain offering.'

content: In the peace offering, what kind of animal may be offered?
explanation: Leviticus 3:1 — 'If his offering is a sacrifice of peace offering... whether male or female, he shall offer it without blemish before the LORD.'

content: What two things were Israelites forbidden to eat from the peace offering?
explanation: Leviticus 3:17 — 'It shall be a statute forever throughout your generations, in all your dwelling places, that you eat neither fat nor blood.'

content: When the anointed priest sins, what animal must he offer as a sin offering?
explanation: Leviticus 4:3 — 'If it is the anointed priest who sins, thus bringing guilt on the people, then he shall offer for the sin that he has committed a bull from the herd without blemish to the LORD for a sin offering.'

content: When a leader sins, what animal must he offer as a sin offering?
explanation: Leviticus 4:22-23 — 'When a leader sins... he shall bring as his offering a goat, a male without blemish.' Different sacrifices vary based on the offender's status.

content: When a common person sins, what animal must he offer?
explanation: Leviticus 4:28 — 'He shall bring for his offering a goat, a female without blemish, for his sin which he has committed.'

content: If the sinner is too poor for birds, what may he offer as a sin offering?
options: A tenth of an ephah of fine flour
explanation: Leviticus 5:11 — 'But if he cannot afford two turtledoves or two pigeons, then he shall bring as his offering for the sin that he has committed a tenth of an ephah of fine flour for a sin offering.'

content: When sinning against holy things, what additional restitution was required?
explanation: Leviticus 5:16 — 'He shall make restitution for what he has done amiss in the holy thing and shall add a fifth to it.'

content: If someone defrauds his neighbor of property, besides the guilt offering, how much must he repay?
explanation: Leviticus 6:5 — 'He shall restore it in full and shall add a fifth to it, and give it to him to whom it belongs on the day he realizes his guilt.'

content: How was the fire on the altar of burnt offering to be kept?
options: Continually burning, never to go out
explanation: Leviticus 6:13 — 'Fire shall be kept burning on the altar continually; it shall not go out.'

content: Who eats the meat of the sin offering?
explanation: Leviticus 6:26 — 'The priest who offers it for sin shall eat it. In a holy place it shall be eaten, in the court of the tent of meeting.'

content: How long must the meat of the thank offering be eaten?
options: On the day it is offered, leaving none until morning
explanation: Leviticus 7:15 — 'And the flesh of the sacrifice of his peace offerings for thanksgiving shall be eaten on the day of his offering. He shall not leave any of it until the morning.'

content: According to Leviticus 7:26-27, what was Israel's rule about eating blood?
explanation: Leviticus 7:26-27 — 'You shall eat no blood whatever, whether of fowl or of animal, in any of your dwelling places. Whoever eats any blood, that person shall be cut off from his people.'
options: Could eat blood of any creature
Absolutely forbidden — whoever ate would be cut off

content: What was the first step in the ordination of Aaron and his sons?
explanation: Leviticus 8:6 — 'And Moses brought Aaron and his sons and washed them with water.' This was the first step in the ordination ceremony.

content: Moses poured the anointing oil on whose head to consecrate him?
explanation: Leviticus 8:12 — 'And he poured some of the anointing oil on Aaron's head and anointed him to consecrate him.'

content: How many days did the ordination of Aaron and his sons last?
explanation: Leviticus 8:33 — 'You shall not go outside the entrance of the tent of meeting for seven days, until the days of your ordination are completed.'

content: What happened when Aaron offered the first sacrifice after the ordination?
options: Fire from the LORD consumed the offering on the altar
A cloud covered the tent of meeting
explanation: Leviticus 9:24 — 'And fire came out from before the LORD and consumed the burnt offering and the pieces of fat on the altar.' The people saw, shouted, and fell on their faces.

content: When the fire from the LORD consumed the offering, what did all the people do?
explanation: Leviticus 9:24 — 'And when all the people saw it, they shouted and fell on their faces.'
options: Shouted and fell on their faces

content: Which two sons of Aaron offered strange fire before the LORD?
explanation: Leviticus 10:1 — 'Now Nadab and Abihu, the sons of Aaron, each took his censer and put fire in it and laid incense on it and offered unauthorized fire before the LORD.'

content: Nadab and Abihu were consumed by ___ from the LORD for offering strange fire.
explanation: Leviticus 10:2 — 'And fire came out from before the LORD and consumed them, and they died before the LORD.'

content: What kind of animals were considered clean to eat?
options: Animals with split hooves that chew the cud
explanation: Leviticus 11:3 — 'Whatever parts the hoof and is cloven-footed and chews the cud, among the animals, you may eat.'

content: Why was the pig considered unclean under Levitical law?
explanation: Leviticus 11:7 — 'And the pig, because it parts the hoof and is cloven-footed but does not chew the cud, is unclean to you.'
options: Because it has split hooves but does not chew the cud
Because of its bristly black hair

content: What must water creatures have to be considered clean?
explanation: Leviticus 11:9 — 'Of all that are in the seas or in the rivers, all that have fins and scales... you may eat.'

content: On what day after birth must a male child be circumcised?
explanation: Leviticus 12:3 — 'And on the eighth day the flesh of his foreskin shall be circumcised.'

content: How much longer is the time of purification when a mother bears a daughter compared to a son?
explanation: Leviticus 12:2,5 — Bearing a son: 7 days unclean + 33 days purifying = 40 days. Bearing a daughter: 14 + 66 = 80 days. Double the time.

content: Who has authority to examine and pronounce someone leprous?
explanation: Leviticus 13:2 — 'Then he shall be brought to Aaron the priest or to one of his sons the priests.'

content: If the priest is uncertain about leprosy, how long must the patient be quarantined?
explanation: Leviticus 13:4 — 'The priest shall shut up the diseased person for seven days.' After that, a re-examination is made.

content: What must a leper cry out when meeting others?
explanation: Leviticus 13:45 — 'The leprous person who has the disease shall wear torn clothes and let the hair of his head hang loose, and he shall cover his upper lip and cry out, Unclean, unclean!'

content: In the cleansing ritual for a leper, what materials are needed?
options: A young bull, blood, and fire
Two birds, cedar wood, scarlet yarn, and hyssop
Two lambs, olive oil, and fine flour
explanation: Leviticus 14:4 — 'The priest shall command them to take for him who is to be cleansed two live clean birds and cedarwood and scarlet yarn and hyssop.'

content: To what does the Levitical leprosy law apply?
explanation: Leviticus 14:34-35 — 'When you come into the land of Canaan... and I put a case of leprous disease in a house... then he who owns the house shall come and tell the priest.' Leprosy applied to houses too.
options: Both to people and to houses

content: After healing, how many days must one with a discharge wait before being cleansed?
explanation: Leviticus 15:13 — 'When the one with a discharge is cleansed of his discharge, he shall count for himself seven days for his cleansing.'

content: What is the main purpose of the cleanliness laws in Leviticus 15?
options: To distinguish Israel from other nations
To keep the Israelites separate from uncleanness, lest they die
explanation: Leviticus 15:31 — 'Thus you shall keep the people of Israel separate from their uncleanness, lest they die in their uncleanness by defiling my tabernacle that is in their midst.'

content: How many times a year did the high priest enter the Most Holy Place?
options: Only once, on the Day of Atonement
explanation: Leviticus 16:2 — 'Tell Aaron your brother not to come at any time into the Holy Place inside the veil... so that he may not die.' Hebrews 9:7 confirms only once a year on the Day of Atonement.

content: On the Day of Atonement, what did the high priest wear when entering the Most Holy Place?
options: Full vestments with ephod and breastpiece
explanation: Leviticus 16:4 — 'He shall put on the holy linen coat and shall have the linen undergarment on his body, and he shall tie the linen sash around his waist, and wear the linen turban.' Not the full priestly vestments.

content: On the Day of Atonement, Aaron cast lots on two goats: one for the LORD and one for whom?
explanation: Leviticus 16:8 — 'And Aaron shall cast lots over the two goats, one lot for the LORD and the other lot for Azazel.'

content: Aaron laid both his hands on the head of the live goat and confessed all the ___ of the people of Israel over it.
explanation: Leviticus 16:21 — 'And Aaron shall lay both his hands on the head of the live goat, and confess over it all the iniquities of the people of Israel, and all their transgressions, all their sins.'

content: The Day of Atonement is observed on the tenth day of the ___ month.
explanation: Leviticus 16:29 — 'It shall be a statute to you forever that in the seventh month, on the tenth day of the month, you shall afflict yourselves.'

content: Where is the life of the flesh?
explanation: Leviticus 17:11 — 'For the life of the flesh is in the blood, and I have given it for you on the altar to make atonement for your souls.'

content: According to Leviticus 17:11, where is the life of the flesh, and what is the blood used for?
explanation: Leviticus 17:11 — 'The life of the flesh is in the blood... blood makes atonement for the soul.' Hebrews 9:22 affirms: 'Without the shedding of blood there is no forgiveness of sins.'
options: In the bones / for animal sacrifices
In the blood / to make atonement for the soul on the altar

content: God forbade Israel to follow the customs of which two lands?
explanation: Leviticus 18:3 — 'You shall not do as they do in the land of Egypt, where you lived, and you shall not do as they do in the land of Canaan, to which I am bringing you.'

content: Israel was forbidden to offer their children to which god?
explanation: Leviticus 18:21 — 'You shall not give any of your children to offer them to Molech, and so profane the name of your God.'

content: You shall be holy, for I the LORD your God am ___.
explanation: Leviticus 19:2 — 'You shall be holy, for I the LORD your God am holy.' This is the central command of Leviticus, echoed in 1 Peter 1:16.

content: When harvesting, Israelites were to leave the corners of their fields and not glean — for whom?
explanation: Leviticus 19:9-10 — 'You shall leave them for the poor and for the sojourner: I am the LORD your God.'

content: You shall love your ___ as yourself.
explanation: Leviticus 19:18 — 'You shall love your neighbor as yourself: I am the LORD.' Jesus quoted this as the second great commandment (Matthew 22:39).

content: What does Leviticus 19:28 forbid Israel from doing to their bodies?
explanation: Leviticus 19:28 — 'You shall not... tattoo yourselves: I am the LORD.' This prohibition relates to pagan rites.

content: What was the punishment for offering one's children to Molech?
explanation: Leviticus 20:2 — 'Any one of the people of Israel or of the strangers who sojourn in Israel who gives any of his children to Molech shall surely be put to death. The people of the land shall stone him with stones.'

content: What command does Leviticus 20:7 give to Israel?
explanation: Leviticus 20:7 — 'Consecrate yourselves, therefore, and be holy, for I am the LORD your God.'
options: Come to the temple every week
Consecrate yourselves and be holy, for I am the LORD your God

content: What is the main reason God distinguished clean and unclean animals for Israel?
options: Because Israel must be holy and set apart for the LORD
explanation: Leviticus 20:26 — 'You shall be holy to me, for I the LORD am holy and have separated you from the peoples, that you should be mine.'

content: What were Aaron's descendants with physical defects allowed to do?
options: Excluded entirely from the priestly line
Serve only outside the tent of meeting
Eat the holy food but not offer sacrifices
Still offer sacrifices on the altar
explanation: Leviticus 21:22 — 'He may eat the bread of his God, both of the most holy and of the holy things, but he shall not go through the veil or approach the altar.'

content: How many days old must a sacrificial animal be at minimum?
explanation: Leviticus 22:27 — 'When an ox or sheep or goat is born, it shall remain seven days with its mother, and from the eighth day on it shall be acceptable as a food offering to the LORD.'

content: What does the LORD command about His holy name in Leviticus 22:32?
explanation: Leviticus 22:32 — 'You shall not profane my holy name, that I may be sanctified among the people of Israel. I am the LORD who sanctifies you.'
options: Do not profane My holy name; I must be sanctified among the people of Israel
It must be carved in stone

content: On what day was the Passover celebrated?
options: First day of the first month
Fifteenth day of the third month
Fourteenth day of the first month
Tenth day of the seventh month
explanation: Leviticus 23:5 — 'In the first month, on the fourteenth day of the month at twilight, is the LORD's Passover.'

content: During the Feast of Unleavened Bread, what did Israel eat for seven days?
explanation: Leviticus 23:6 — 'And on the fifteenth day of the same month is the Feast of Unleavened Bread to the LORD; for seven days you shall eat unleavened bread.'

content: How many days after the Passover was the Feast of Weeks (Pentecost) celebrated?
explanation: Leviticus 23:15-16 — 'You shall count seven full weeks... You shall count fifty days.' This is the origin of the name 'Pentecost' (50 days).

content: The first day of the seventh month is a day of solemn rest, a memorial proclaimed with a blast of ___.
explanation: Leviticus 23:24 — 'In the seventh month, on the first day of the month, you shall observe a day of solemn rest, a memorial proclaimed with blast of trumpets.' This is the Feast of Trumpets (Rosh Hashanah).

content: How many days does the Feast of Booths last?
explanation: Leviticus 23:34 — 'On the fifteenth day of this seventh month and for seven days is the Feast of Booths to the LORD.'

content: What does the Feast of Booths remind Israel of?
options: The creation of heaven and earth
The time of dwelling in booths during the exodus from Egypt
explanation: Leviticus 23:43 — 'That your generations may know that I made the people of Israel dwell in booths when I brought them out of the land of Egypt.'

content: How many loaves of bread were placed on the table before the LORD?
explanation: Leviticus 24:5-6 — 'You shall take fine flour and bake twelve loaves from it... You shall set them in two piles, six in a pile, on the table of pure gold before the LORD.'

content: What was the penalty for blaspheming the name of the LORD?
explanation: Leviticus 24:16 — 'Whoever blasphemes the name of the LORD shall surely be put to death. All the congregation shall stone him.'
options: Banished from the camp for seven days
Put to death — the whole congregation must stone him

content: Where in Leviticus is the law of 'eye for eye, tooth for tooth' recorded?
explanation: Leviticus 24:20 — 'Fracture for fracture, eye for eye, tooth for tooth; whatever injury he has given a person shall be given to him.'

content: How often is the Sabbath year of rest for the land?
explanation: Leviticus 25:4 — 'But in the seventh year there shall be a Sabbath of solemn rest for the land, a Sabbath to the LORD.'

content: How often is the Year of Jubilee celebrated?
explanation: Leviticus 25:10-11 — 'You shall consecrate the fiftieth year... That fiftieth year shall be a jubilee for you.'

content: What theological significance does the Year of Jubilee in Leviticus 25 have in relation to the New Testament?
options: A type of the final judgment
A type of liberation and freedom in Jesus (Luke 4:18-19)
explanation: Leviticus 25:10 — 'Proclaim liberty throughout the land to all its inhabitants.' Jesus quoted Isaiah 61:1-2 (built on Jubilee) in Luke 4:18-19 to declare His mission of liberation.

content: According to Leviticus 25:36-37, could an Israelite lend money to a fellow Israelite at interest?
explanation: Leviticus 25:36-37 — 'Take no interest from him or profit, but fear your God... You shall not lend him your money at interest, nor give him your food for profit.'
options: Yes, if the interest was small
No interest or profit may be taken from a brother

content: What blessing did God promise if Israel kept His commandments?
options: Rain in season and the land yielding produce
explanation: Leviticus 26:3-4 — 'If you walk in my statutes... then I will give you your rains in their season, and the land shall yield its increase, and the trees of the field shall yield their fruit.'

content: I will make my dwelling among you... I will walk among you and will be your ___, and you shall be my people.
explanation: Leviticus 26:12 — 'I will walk among you and will be your God, and you shall be my people.' This covenant promise is echoed in 2 Corinthians 6:16.

content: What is the final consequence if Israel refuses to obey God?
explanation: Leviticus 26:33 — 'And I will scatter you among the nations, and I will unsheathe the sword after you, and your land shall be a desolation, and your cities shall be a waste.'

content: Despite punishment, what does God promise if Israel confesses their sins?
options: He will give them a new land
He will give them a new king
He will remember His covenant with Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob
explanation: Leviticus 26:42 — 'Then I will remember my covenant with Jacob, and I will remember my covenant with Isaac and my covenant with Abraham, and I will remember the land.'

content: When someone vows to dedicate a person's value to the LORD, the highest valuation is for which group?
explanation: Leviticus 27:3 — 'Then the valuation of a male from twenty years old up to sixty years old shall be fifty shekels of silver.' This is the highest valuation in the schedule.

content: Could a thing devoted (cherem) to the LORD be redeemed?
explanation: Leviticus 27:28 — 'But no devoted thing that a man devotes to the LORD, of anything that he has... shall be sold or redeemed; every devoted thing is most holy to the LORD.'
options: Yes, with double its value in money
No — it cannot be sold or redeemed; it is most holy to the LORD

content: To whom does the tithe of the land belong?
explanation: Leviticus 27:30 — 'Every tithe of the land, whether of the seed of the land or of the fruit of the trees, is the LORD's; it is holy to the LORD.'
